summary: Gruber reflects on the eve of WWDC 2010, noting that the most striking thing isn't any specific rumor or announcement but how dramatically Apple's fortunes have changed in just one year. He recalls that at WWDC 2009, the iPad was a distant rumor and Steve Jobs was on medical leave recovering from a liver transplant. Now the iPad is a smash hit, Apple's market cap has surpassed Microsoft's, and Jobs is back at the top of his game. It's a brief, emotional snapshot of Apple at a peak moment.
key_points: The most remarkable aspect of WWDC 2010 isn't any single product announcement but the totality of Apple's transformation over the prior year
In just twelve months, Apple went from uncertainty about Jobs's health to him being back in his prime on stage
The iPad went from distant rumor to industry-changing hit, and Apple's market cap surpassed Microsoft's
money_quotes: What strikes me about this year's WWDC isn't any specific rumor or impending announcement.
One year ago I was in the same hotel waiting for the same keynote to the same conference.
The iPad was a distant rumor, and Steve Jobs was on medical leave, two months out from a life-saving liver transplant.

Now here we are, and the iPad is an amazing industry-changing smash hit (I'm writing these words using one), Apple's market cap has surpassed Microsoft's, and Steve Jobs is getting ready to take the stage, in his prime and at the top of his game.
It's been one hell of a year.
key_insight: Sometimes the most powerful story isn't about any single product but about the arc of an entire year that transformed a company's trajectory and its leader's return from the brink.
